_usage: Article template for every blog post (/blog/{post-slug}). Emit one per article. Filled here from the seed article 'ReBAC vs RBAC for AI agents' (blog.md Part 2). Replace headline, description, url, dates, author, and articleSection per post. `dateModified` is critical for AI freshness signals — update it on every edit (the URL has no date segment, so evergreen posts are refreshed in place per url-taxonomy §4). `author` references a Person via @id (see person.template.json); the same Person @id is reused across that author's posts. `speakable` marks the lead citable-answer block and FAQ for voice/AI extraction — confirm the CSS selectors match the built article's markup. Pair with FAQPage (faqpage.template.json) and BreadcrumbList on the same page; comparison/listicle posts add ItemList for the ranked set. Keep exact terminology and no fabricated metrics.
headline: ReBAC vs RBAC for AI agents: why relationship-based access wins
description: ReBAC vs RBAC for AI agents explained: RBAC grants access by static role and hits role explosion; ReBAC grants access by relationship to the resource, which fits the 'this Agent, for that Operator, on this data' question agentic AI raises.
keywords: ReBAC, RBAC, relationship-based access control, AI agents, agentic AI governance
